sub_topics.title: Melodic Motion

instructional_blocks.hook: Identifying conjunct and disjunct motion in a melody
teacher_steps: Learners to sing any familiar melodies (e.g. Row, row, row your boat) and identify different intervals between the notes of the melody
Play some notes on the keyboard and ask learners in groups to identify them as either conjunct or disjunct motion
Engage learners in singing different melodies and identify conjunct and disjunct motion in the melodies
learner_steps: Sing familiar melodies and identify intervals
Identify notes played on keyboard as conjunct or disjunct
Analyze given melodies and identify conjunct and disjunct motion in groups

instructional_blocks.hook: Identifying the shape/contour of the melody (valleys, waves, hill)
teacher_steps: Learners listen or sing simple common melodies then identify what a valley, wave or a hill
Learners to freely explore and identify the shape/contour of different melodies with clear examples of valley, waves and hills
learner_steps: Listen or sing melodies and identify valley, wave, or hill contours
Explore different melodies identifying their shape/contour

sub_topics.short_notes: Melodic motion refers to the way pitches in rhythm move in relation to each other within a melody, creating a sense of progression and direction. There are two types of melodic motion: conjunct motion, which proceeds by step from one scale degree to the next (i.e., by the interval of a 2nd), and disjunct motion, which proceeds by leap (i.e., by intervals larger than a 2nd). The contour or shape of a melody can be described as valleys (descending), waves (oscillating), or hills (ascending).

vocabulary.definition: Smooth, stepwise movement between consecutive pitches in a melody e.g. (C-D-E)
Movement between consecutive pitches in a melody by larger intervals, often creating a sense of leap or jump, e.g. (C-E, C-G)
The overall shape or pattern of ups and downs in pitch, creating a unique outline or topography
A musical phrase or section that gradually rise in pitch, often creating a sense of tension, anticipation or build up
A musical phrase or section that descends in pitch, often creating a sense of relaxation, resolution, or calmness
A musical phrase or section that moves in a wave like motion

questions: Provide learners with printed melodies and ask them to identify and label the valleys, waves and hills
Give learners a melody with missing notes and ask them to complete the melody by adding notes that create a specific contour
Play a series of melodies with different contours and ask learners to identify the type of contour used in each melody

Study the melody from 'This is What You Came For' and answer: a) In which two bars does the melody move by disjunct movement? b) In which two bars does the melody move by conjunct movement? c) What is the note name of the lowest pitch note? d) Identify the highest pitch note name? e) Measure the interval between lowest and highest note

sub_topics.title: Simple Melody Writing

teacher_steps: Engage learners in small groups and discuss what a motif is in melody writing
Play or sing a well-known melody that features a motif and ask learners to identify and discuss motif in the melody
In smaller groups learners study and identify the motifs provided
Highlight major features of the motif for each melody
Learners give examples of motifs from songs of their choices
With learners, analyse the motifs given as examples
learner_steps: Discuss in small groups what a motif is
Identify motifs in well-known melodies
Study and identify motifs in provided examples
Give examples of motifs from own song choices
Analyze motifs given as examples

instructional_blocks.hook: Distinguishing various phrases in the melody (antecedent/question, consequent/answer)
teacher_steps: Ask learners to discuss what a phrase is and how to distinguish it in a melody
Learners distinguish between an antecedent/question and consequent/answer in a given melody
Encourage exploration among learners by asking them to distinguish between a question and an answer in a melody in groups or individually
Remind learners that melody usually pauses slightly at the end of each phrase
Emphasis should be made that in vocal music, the musical phrases tend to follow the phrases and sentences of the text
learner_steps: Discuss what a phrase is
Distinguish between antecedent and consequent phrases
Explore identifying questions and answers in melodies
Understand that phrases pause slightly at their ends

instructional_blocks.hook: Composing Simple Melodies (At least four bars)
teacher_steps: In smaller groups learners to compose a simple four bar melody

Note that when composing a melody, consider: time signature and key signature, starting scale degree and beat, rhythmic patterns, maintain and vary the motif, provide consequent to antecedent, sing or hum while composing, end on tonic with long note approached in stepwise motion
learner_steps: Compose a simple four bar melody in groups
Consider time signature, key signature, starting note, rhythmic patterns
Maintain and vary motif
Provide consequent phrase to antecedent
End on tonic with stepwise approach

sub_topics.short_notes: Simple melody writing refers to the process or technique of creating a short, coherent, and memorable musical idea using pitch and rhythm, to convey emotion and mood. A motif is a short, repeated pattern or idea, often just a few notes, that is used to build a melody or tune. A phrase is a unit of musical thought consisting of a group of notes that form a coherent and meaningful musical idea. An antecedent phrase is the first phrase in a pair of phrases, typically followed by a consequent phrase. A melody is made up of phrases, which are usually two or four bars long. When composing simple melodies, consider: time signature and key signature, starting scale degree and beat, rhythmic patterns to be used, maintain the motif and vary it where possible, provide a consequent to the antecedent, sing or hum while composing to internalize it, end on the tonic with a long note approached in stepwise motion avoiding unnecessary wide leaps.

vocabulary.definition: A short, repeated pattern or idea, often just a few notes, that is used to build a melody or tune
A group of notes that form a complete musical thought or idea, similar to a sentence in language
The first part of a melodic phrase that sets up a musical idea, often ending in uncertainty, to be resolved by the consequent
Techniques used by composers to create interest, variety and emotional impact in a melody

questions: In smaller groups, learners to complete provided melodies to 'four' bars by writing a consequent phrase
In pairs, learners create and vary a simple melodic motif within four bars
Individually learners compose a 'four' bar melody in any key of their choice
Sight sing or sight read the composed melodies

sub_topics.title: Binary and ternary
competences: Construct melody in binary and ternary forms

teacher_steps: Ask learners to listen, sing or play simple melodies like 'Baba Black sheep' and identify rhythmic and melodic contrast
Promote analytical thinking by allowing learners to listen, sing or play simple melodies and identify rhythmic and melodic contrast
Play a melody with a contrasting contour or rhythm and ask learners to imitate it. Then, ask them to create their own melody
Encourage creativity among the learners by asking them to create their own melody with a contrasting contour
Provide learners with a score or recording a piece. Ask them to analyse the form, identifying areas of contrast and unity
learner_steps: Listen, sing or play simple melodies and identify rhythmic and melodic contrast
Imitate melodies with contrasting contour or rhythm
Create own melody with contrasting contour
Analyze provided scores identifying areas of contrast and unity

instructional_blocks.hook: Constructing sections of equal length of binary and ternary structure
teacher_steps: Ask learners to compose a short piece in binary or ternary form ensuring each section A and B or A, B and A have the equal number of measures (4 measures each)
Put learners in groups so that they work as a team and later on allow them to do the work individually
Encourage critical thinking and creativity as they work on this activity
Class discussion on the characteristics of binary and ternary forms. Ask learners to share their compositions and provide feedback on section length and contrast
Facilitate a class discussion on the characteristics of binary and ternary forms. Ensure that each learner is fully involved in the discussion
learner_steps: Compose a short piece in binary or ternary form with equal measures
Work in groups then individually
Share compositions and receive feedback
Participate in class discussion on binary and ternary characteristics

instructional_blocks.hook: Constructing sections of unequal length in binary and ternary design
teacher_steps: Ask learners to compose a binary form piece (AB) with sections of unequal length (e.g., 4 measures in A and 6 measures in B) or compose a ternary form piece (ABA) with sections of unequal length
Ensure that the constructed measures are not equal
Provide learners with a partially completed binary or ternary form piece. Ask them to expand one section
Ensure that the suggested sections for binary and ternary forms are expanded as expected
Give learners a score in binary or ternary form with unequal length. Ask them to identify and label the sections
Ensure that learners are able to identify and label the sections. Guide the learners throughout the lesson
learner_steps: Compose binary or ternary pieces with unequal section lengths
Expand sections in partially completed pieces
Identify and label sections in provided scores with unequal lengths

sub_topics.short_notes: Form in music refers to the overall structure or organization of a piece of music. It encompasses the arrangement of musical elements such as melody, harmony, rhythm, and tempo to create a cohesive and meaningful whole. Binary form is a simple musical structure consisting of two contrasting sections (AB). The A section typically presents a theme or melody, while the B section presents a contrasting theme or develops the initial theme. The two sections are usually of equal or approximately equal length. Ternary form is a musical structure consisting of three main sections, typically represented as A-B-A. This means the piece begins with an initial theme or section (A), followed by a contrasting middle section (B), and concludes with a return to the initial theme or section (A). Ternary form provides a sense of balance and symmetry, making it a popular choice for composers and songwriters.

vocabulary.definition: A simple musical structure consisting of two contrasting sections (AB)
A musical structure consisting of three sections, with the first and third sections being similar (ABA)
A recurring melody or idea
A distinct part of the music, often with its own unique characteristics
A passage connecting two sections
A repetition pattern of pitches at a higher or lower level
Repeating a melodic idea or pattern
Changing a melodic idea or pattern while maintaining its core identity

questions: Prepare tasks to be administered during and after learning sessions and give carry home tasks
Form and analysis – Analyse and identify the form of provided melodies
Written and practical assessment (composing and performing melodies in binary or ternary form)

sub_topics.title: Italian Terms
competences: Interpret the Italian terms for tempo
Interpret the Italian terms for dynamics

instructional_blocks.hook: Interpreting the Italian terms for tempo (allegro, presto, lento)
teacher_steps: Divide learners in groups and give them a musical score sheet with Italian terms. Ask them to interpret as well as demonstrate how the music will be performed
Engage learners to perform popular music (songs) and apply different tempo
Learners to listen to piece of different tempo
learner_steps: Interpret Italian tempo terms in provided musical scores
Demonstrate how music with different tempo markings should be performed
Perform popular songs applying different tempos
Listen to pieces with different tempos

instructional_blocks.hook: Interpreting the Italian terms for dynamics (piano, forte)
teacher_steps: Divide learners in groups and give them a musical score sheet with Italian terms for dynamics. Ask them to interpret as well as demonstrate how the music will be performed
Engage learners to interpret the Italian terms for dynamics and help them demonstrate how they are applied either by singing or playing instruments as prescribed (Soft, loud)
learner_steps: Interpret Italian dynamic terms in provided musical scores
Demonstrate how music with different dynamic markings should be performed
Apply dynamics by singing or playing instruments (soft, loud, crescendo, etc.)

sub_topics.short_notes: Musical terms and signs are specialized words and symbols used in music notation to convey information about pitch, rhythm, dynamics, articulation, and other aspects of musical performance. Most musical terms are written in Italian Language because of the country's significant contributions to the development of Western classical music. Italian composers were the first to write 'instruction' words at certain points in their music. Italian musical terms have become an integral part of the language of music, providing a universal vocabulary for musicians to communicate and interpret music accurately. The term 'tempo' is derived from the Italian language. In Italian, 'tempo' means 'time' and in music, it refers to the speed or rate at which a piece of music is performed. In music, dynamics refer to the varying degrees of loudness and softness in a musical composition. It describes the contrast between quiet and loud sounds, which adds expression, emotion, and depth to the music.

vocabulary.definition: Getting quicker
Leisurely – usually quite slow
(cheerful) fast
(easy going) At a walking pace
Moderate tempo (moderately)
Very fast
holding back
Full of life, fast, lively tempo
Very soft
Soft
Moderately soft
moderately loud
Loud
Very loud
Gradually getting louder
getting softer

questions: Sight reading/singing
Playing instruments
Listening and analysis
Written work

sub_topics.title: Musical Signs
competences: Demonstrate musical signs in music composition

instructional_blocks.hook: Identifying music signs (staccato, legato, repeat signs)
teacher_steps: Learners in groups to identify music signs (staccato, legato, repeat signs) on a music score provided
Guide the learners to explore and identify music signs (staccato, legato, repeat signs) on a music score provided
learner_steps: Identify music signs in provided musical scores in groups
Explore and recognize staccato, legato, and repeat signs

teacher_steps: Perform melodies in groups demonstrating (staccato, legato, repeat signs) on a music score provided
Facilitate group performances to help learners demonstrate (staccato, legato, repeat signs)
learner_steps: Perform melodies demonstrating staccato articulation
Perform melodies demonstrating legato articulation
Perform melodies observing repeat signs

sub_topics.short_notes: Musical signs also known as musical notation symbols, are graphical representations used to convey musical information. They help musicians read and interpret musical score. The word staccato comes from the Italian word meaning 'detached' or 'disconnected,' which means to play notes short and sharply, with a slight silence between each note. It is indicated by a small dot (.) placed either above or below the note head, signifying that the note should be played short and detached. Legato is an Italian term meaning tied together. Legato is a performance technique used in music. It creates a smooth, steady flow between notes. Every single note is played to its utmost length and then harmonizes straight into the following note, allowing a better crescendo or decrescendo of the phrase. In music, a repeat sign is symbol that indicate a section of music should be repeated.

vocabulary.definition: Play notes short and sharply, with a slight silence between each note, indicated by a small dot (.) placed either above or below the note head
An Italian term meaning tied together; creates a smooth, steady flow between notes where every single note is played to its utmost length
Symbols that indicate a section of music should be repeated

questions: Sight reading/singing
Playing instruments
Listening and analysis
Written work (composition)

sub_topics.title: Triads

teacher_steps: Ask learners to identify and label the triads as root, third and fifth on a staff using the tonic triad in C major
Facilitate learners' involvement in identifying and labelling the triads as root, third and fifth
Break learners into smaller groups to discuss and identify the triads as root, third and fifth
Ask learners to either sing or play the constructed tonic triad in C major
Allow learners in groups to freely explore and experiment by either singing or playing the tonic triad in C major
learner_steps: Identify and label triads as root, third, and fifth on staff
Discuss in groups how to identify triads
Sing or play the tonic triad in C major
Explore and experiment with triads in groups

teacher_steps: Engage learners in constructing triads (root, 3rd, 5th) on the staff in different keys
Engage and support learners in constructing triads (root, 5th, 3rd) on either treble or bass staff
learner_steps: Construct triads on staff in different keys
Practice constructing triads on treble and bass staff

instructional_blocks.hook: Describing triads (major, minor, diminished or augmented)
teacher_steps: Ask learners to describe triads as (major, minor, diminished or augmented)
Introduce the theoretical and practical application of triads as (major, minor, diminished or augmented)
Introduce the basics of triads-a triad is a three-note chord built from a root, 3rd and 5th
Guide learners how to identify intervals in a triad e.g. Major 3rd which has 3 steps with 4 semitones (C to E) and minor 3rd has 3 steps with 3 semitones (C to E flat)
Major triad structure is root + major third + perfect fifth
Minor triad structure has root + minor third + perfect fifth
learner_steps: Describe triads as major, minor, diminished, or augmented
Learn to identify intervals within triads
Understand major and minor triad structures
Apply theoretical knowledge to practical examples

teacher_steps: Invert triads from root position to first and second inversion
Guide learners on how to invert triads from root, first and second inversion
Provide worksheets where learners must label triads and their inversions in musical notation
Have learners practice performing triads and their inversions on their instruments
Start with simple major and minor triads before moving to diminished and augmented
learner_steps: Invert triads from root to first inversion
Invert triads from first to second inversion
Label triads and their inversions in notation
Perform triads and inversions on instruments

teacher_steps: Perform each type of triad (Major, minor, diminished and augmented) on an instrument (piano, guitar)
Guide learners how to play triads on an instrument
Give learners a root note and have them play the third and the fifth notes
Engage learners in ear training by identifying the played triads (Major, minor)
Comparative listening-guide: learners to play in pairs e.g. major vs minor and have students describe emotional quality of each
learner_steps: Perform major, minor, diminished, and augmented triads on instruments
Practice playing triads given only the root note
Develop ear training by identifying triads by sound
Compare and describe emotional qualities of different triad types

instructional_blocks.hook: Identifying primary and secondary triads in any given scale
teacher_steps: Learners to identify primary and secondary triads in groups in any given scale
Carefully guide learners how to identify primary and secondary triads in any given scale
learner_steps: Identify primary triads (I, IV, V) in given scales
Identify secondary triads (ii, iii, vi, vii°) in given scales
Work in groups to identify all triads in various scales

instructional_blocks.hook: Applying primary and secondary triads in any given scale
teacher_steps: Learners construct primary and secondary triads in any given scale/key
Guide learners how to construct primary and secondary triads in any given key/scale
learner_steps: Construct primary triads in given keys
Construct secondary triads in given keys
Apply knowledge to create chord progressions

sub_topics.short_notes: In music, Harmony refers to the combination of different musical notes played or sung at the same time to produce a pleasing sound. It usually supports the melody (the tune) and adds depth, emotion and texture to a piece of music. Harmony is created when two or more notes sound together, forming chords (like C major or G minor), or more complex structures. A triad is a three-note chord built from a root, third and fifth. It is a group of three notes played or sung simultaneously. When we refer to the root, third and fifth, we mean that: The root is a base note (foundation note), The third is a third interval above the root, The fifth is a fifth note (a perfect 5th) above the root. There are four main types of triads: Major triad (root + major third + perfect fifth), Minor triad (root + minor third + perfect fifth), Diminished triad (root + minor third + diminished fifth), and Augmented triad (root + major third + augmented fifth). Primary triads are the I, IV, and V chords built on the first, fourth, and fifth scale degrees. Secondary triads are built on the remaining scale degrees (ii, iii, vi, vii°).

vocabulary.definition: Is formed when two or more different notes are played or sung together. The most common is a Triad, which consists of 3 notes (C-E-G)
A basic three-note chord made of root, third and fifth (D minor triad=D-F-A)
The distance between two notes. Common intervals include 2nd, 3rd, 5th and Octaves
A chord where the root is not the lowest note. Changes the feel/sound of the chord
A combination of notes that sounds stable, pleasant or resolved
A combination of notes that sounds tense or unstable; often used to create drama or resolution
A sequence of chords played in successions (I - IV - V - I)
A chord progression that signals the end of a phrase or section (perfect cadence V-I)
Exploring different ways to arrange notes in chords can lead to fuller or more varied sound such as using inversions

questions: Identify, construct and play/sing the triads given
Create a quiz to test learners' ability to identify triads
Assign a project where learners analyse and identify triads in selected score
Give short answer questions by asking students to explain triad concepts and give musical examples
Ask learners to compose short pieces of music using triads
Create a quiz to test learners' ability to understand primary and secondary triads in any given key/scale
Assign a project where learners analyse and identify primary and secondary triads in any given key/scale
Ask learners to create and notate simple chord progressions of primary and secondary triads in any given key/scale
